html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twenty — Part Three

The Twelve Minor Prophets

OVADYAH · YONAH · MIKHAH · NAKHUM

4QXII — Dead Sea Scrolls Scroll of the Twelve Prophets

Four Prophets of Covenant Justice and Mercy

The Kingdom Shall Be YHWH's — Should I Not Pity Nineveh — Do Justice Love Kindness Walk Humbly — There Is No Healing for Your Wound

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

4QXII — Dead Sea Scrolls — Cave 4 Qumran — The Scroll of the Twelve

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

OVADYAH — THE KINGDOM SHALL BE YHWH'S

OVADYAH — CHAPTER 1 The Vision of Ovadyah — Though You Soar Like the Eagle — The Day of YHWH Is Near Upon All the Nations — The House of Yaakov Shall Be a Fire — The Kingdom Shall Be YHWH's

1 The vision of Ovadyah. Thus says the Creator YHWH concerning Edom: We have heard a report from YHWH, and a messenger has been sent among the nations. Rise up. Let us rise against her for battle.

2 Behold, I will make you small among the nations. You shall be utterly despised.

3 The pride of your heart has deceived you, you who live in the clefts of the rock, in your lofty dwelling, who say in your heart, Who will bring me down to the ground?

4 Though you soar aloft like the eagle, though your nest is set among the stars, from there I will bring you down, declares YHWH.

5 If thieves came to you, if plunderers came by night — how you have been destroyed — would they not steal only enough for themselves? If grape gatherers came to you, would they not leave gleanings?

6 How Esav has been pillaged, his treasures sought out.

7 All your allies have driven you to your border. Those at peace with you have deceived you. They have prevailed against you. Those who eat your bread have set a trap beneath you. You have no understanding.

8 Will I not on that day, declares YHWH, destroy the wise men out of Edom, and understanding out of Mount Esav?

9 And your mighty men shall be dismayed, O Teman, so that every man from Mount Esav will be cut off by slaughter.

10 Because of the violence done to your brother Yaakov, shame shall cover you, and you shall be cut off forever.

11 On the day that you stood aloof, on the day that strangers carried off his wealth and foreigners entered his gates and cast lots for Yerushalayim, you were like one of them.

12 But do not gloat over the day of your brother in the day of his misfortune. Do not rejoice over the people of Yehudah in the day of their ruin. Do not boast in the day of distress.

13 Do not enter the gate of my people in the day of their calamity. Do not gloat over his disaster in the day of his calamity. Do not loot his wealth in the day of his calamity.

14 Do not stand at the crossroads to cut off his fugitives. Do not hand over his survivors in the day of distress.

15 For the day of YHWH is near upon all the nations. As you have done, it shall be done to you. Your deeds shall return on your own head.

16 For as you have drunk on my holy mountain, so all the nations shall drink continually. They shall drink and swallow, and shall be as though they had never been.

17 But in Mount Tziyon there shall be those who escape, and it shall be holy, and the house of Yaakov shall possess their own possessions.

18 The house of Yaakov shall be a fire, and the house of Yosef a flame, and the house of Esav stubble. They shall burn them and consume them, and there shall be no survivor for the house of Esav, for YHWH has spoken.

19 Those of the Negev shall possess Mount Esav, and those of the Shephelah shall possess the land of the Pelishtim. They shall possess the land of Efrayim and the land of Samaria, and Binyamin shall possess Gil'ad.

20 The exiles of this host of the people of Yisra'EL shall possess the land of the Kena'anim as far as Tzarfat, and the exiles of Yerushalayim who are in Sefarad shall possess the cities of the Negev.

21 Saviors shall go up to Mount Tziyon to rule Mount Esav, and the kingdom shall be YHWH's.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Ovadyah as the shortest book in the Twelve. The oracle against Edom draws on the covenant historical memory of Edom's behavior at the fall of Yerushalayim — standing aloof, gloating, blocking fugitives, participating in the plunder. Verse 15 — as you have done, it shall be done to you — is the covenant lex talionis applied at the national covenant level: Edom's treatment of the covenant people becomes the covenant measure of Edom's own covenant judgment.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Ovadyah in full. Verse 21 — saviors shall go up to Mount Tziyon to rule Mount Esav, and the kingdom shall be YHWH's. The covenant conclusion of the shortest prophetic book is the covenant climax: YHWH's covenant sovereignty over all kingdoms is the covenant final word. The covenant conflict between Yaakov and Esav that began in the womb — Bereshit 25 — ends in YHWH's covenant kingdom.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Ovadyah. Verse 3-4 — the pride of your heart has deceived you, you who live in the clefts of the rock. Though you soar like the eagle and set your nest among the stars, from there I will bring you down. The covenant height-judgment principle stated with covenant geographic precision: Edom's cliff-dwelling pride — Petra and the Negev highlands — is the covenant image of pride that reaches toward heaven and is brought down by YHWH.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10-14 — the covenant catalog of Edom's sins of omission and commission against Yehudah at the fall: stood aloof — verse 11 — gloated — verse 12 — entered the gates — verse 13 — looted — verse 13 — cut off fugitives — verse 14 — handed over survivors — verse 14. The covenant accusation is specific and sequential. Edom did not merely fail to help — it actively participated in the covenant destruction of its covenant brother at every stage of the covenant catastrophe.

YONAH — SHOULD I NOT PITY NINEVEH

YONAH — CHAPTER 1 Arise and Go to Nineveh — Yonah Fled to Tarshish — A Great Storm — Cast Me Into the Sea — The Men Feared YHWH Exceedingly — A Great Fish

1 Now the word of YHWH came to Yonah the son of Amittai, saying,

2 Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city, and call out against it, for their evil has come up before me.

3 But Yonah rose to flee to Tarshish from the presence of YHWH. He went down to Yapho and found a ship going to Tarshish. So he paid the fare and went down into it, to go with them to Tarshish, away from the presence of YHWH.

4 But YHWH hurled a great wind upon the sea, and there was a mighty tempest on the sea, so that the ship threatened to break up.

5 Then the mariners were afraid, and each cried out to his god. And they hurled the cargo that was in the ship into the sea to lighten it for them. But Yonah had gone down into the inner part of the ship and had lain down and was fast asleep.

6 So the captain came and said to him, What do you mean, you sleeper? Arise, call out to your god. Perhaps the god will give a thought to us, that we may not perish.

7 And they said to one another, Come, let us cast lots, that we may know on whose account this evil has come upon us. So they cast lots, and the lot fell on Yonah.

8 Then they said to him, Tell us on whose account this evil has come upon us. What is your occupation? And where do you come from? What is your country? And of what people are you?

9 And he said to them, I am a Hebrew, and I fear YHWH, the Creator of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land.

10 Then the men were exceedingly afraid and said to him, What is this that you have done? For the men knew that he was fleeing from the presence of YHWH, because he had told them.

11 Then they said to him, What shall we do to you, that the sea may quiet down for us? For the sea grew more and more tempestuous.

12 He said to them, Pick me up and hurl me into the sea. Then the sea will quiet down for you, for I know it is because of me that this great tempest has come upon you.

13 Nevertheless, the men rowed hard to get back to dry land, but they could not, for the sea grew more and more tempestuous against them.

14 Therefore they called out to YHWH, O YHWH, let us not perish for this man's life, and lay not on us innocent blood, for you, O YHWH, have done as it pleased you.

15 So they picked up Yonah and hurled him into the sea, and the sea ceased from its raging.

16 Then the men feared YHWH exceedingly, and they offered a sacrifice to YHWH and made vows.

17 And YHWH appointed a great fish to swallow up Yonah. And Yonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Yonah as part of the Twelve. The covenant irony of Chapter 1 is total: the covenant prophet who fears YHWH — verse 9 — and knows the sea is YHWH's — verse 9 — flees by sea from YHWH. The pagan sailors pray to their gods while the covenant prophet sleeps. The pagan sailors try to save Yonah's life — verse 13 — while the covenant prophet has already consigned himself to death. The covenant outsiders demonstrate covenant virtues that the covenant prophet abandons.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Yonah Chapter 1 in full. Verse 16 — the men feared YHWH exceedingly, and they offered a sacrifice to YHWH and made vows. The covenant conversion of the pagan sailors through the covenant crisis: the pagan mariners arrive at genuine covenant fear of YHWH through the covenant events surrounding Yonah's flight. The covenant prophet's covenant failure produces covenant faith in the covenant outsiders.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 1. Verse 3 — Yonah rose to flee to Tarshish from the presence of YHWH. He went down to Yapho. The covenant theological geography of flight: Yonah goes down — Yapho is below Yerushalayim — down into the ship — verse 3 — down into the inner part of the ship — verse 5. The covenant descent from the covenant presence is consistently downward. The covenant flight from YHWH always goes down.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — I am a Hebrew, and I fear YHWH, the Creator of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land. The covenant identification that makes the flight absurd: the covenant prophet declares that YHWH made the very sea he is fleeing upon. The covenant confession of YHWH's covenant sovereignty is simultaneously the covenant acknowledgment that the flight is covenant impossible. Yonah knows YHWH cannot be fled and flees anyway — the most honest portrait of covenant irrational covenant rebellion in the canon.

YONAH — CHAPTER 2 Yonah's Prayer From the Belly of the Fish — Out of the Belly of Sheol I Cried — You Cast Me Into the Deep — I Will Sacrifice With the Voice of Thanksgiving

1 Then Yonah prayed to YHWH his Creator from the belly of the fish,

2 Saying, I called out to YHWH, out of my distress, and he answered me. Out of the belly of Sheol I cried, and you heard my voice.

3 For you cast me into the deep, into the heart of the seas, and the flood surrounded me. All your waves and your billows passed over me.

4 Then I said, I am driven away from your sight. Yet I shall again look upon your holy temple.

5 The waters closed in over me to take my life. The deep surrounded me. Weeds were wrapped about my head

6 At the roots of the mountains. I went down to the land whose bars closed upon me forever. Yet you brought up my life from the pit, O YHWH my Creator.

7 When my life was fainting away, I remembered YHWH, and my prayer came to you, into your holy temple.

8 Those who pay regard to vain idols forsake their hope of steadfast love.

9 But I with the voice of thanksgiving will sacrifice to you. What I have vowed I will pay. Salvation belongs to YHWH.

10 And YHWH spoke to the fish, and it vomited Yonah out upon the dry land.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Yonah Chapter 2 in close agreement with the Masoretic. The psalm from the fish belly is the covenant prayer of the covenant prophet in the covenant lowest place: the belly of the fish is simultaneously Sheol — the covenant underworld — and the covenant womb of re-commissioning. The covenant vocabulary of the psalm draws on the covenant Tehillim tradition: the covenant imagery of drowning, the covenant cry heard in the covenant sanctuary, the covenant vow of covenant thanksgiving.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 9 — salvation belongs to YHWH. The covenant theological declaration that closes the covenant psalm: covenant salvation is YHWH's sole covenant prerogative. Yonah, who has tried to manage his own covenant itinerary, arrives in the fish belly at the covenant acknowledgment that salvation is not his to control or withhold.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 2. Verse 4 — I am driven away from your sight. Yet I shall again look upon your holy temple. The covenant tension of the psalm: Yonah acknowledges covenant separation from YHWH's covenant presence — driven from your sight — while simultaneously asserting covenant hope of covenant restoration — yet I shall again look. The covenant prayer in the fish belly maintains covenant hope in covenant impossibility.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — when my life was fainting away, I remembered YHWH. The covenant anthropology of covenant memory: Yonah remembers YHWH at the point of death, not before. The covenant rebellion that produced the flight continued until the covenant extremity of the fish belly produced covenant memory. The covenant extremity of the fish belly is the covenant mercy of YHWH: without it, the covenant prophet would not have remembered. Covenant extremity is the covenant gift that restores covenant memory.

YONAH — CHAPTER 3 Arise and Go to Nineveh — Yonah Went and Cried Out — Yet Forty Days and Nineveh Shall Be Overthrown — The People of Nineveh Believed Creator — The King Covered Himself With Sackcloth — Creator Relented

1 Then the word of YHWH came to Yonah the second time, saying,

2 Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city, and call out against it the message that I tell you.

3 So Yonah arose and went to Nineveh, according to the word of YHWH. Now Nineveh was an exceedingly great city, three days' journey in breadth.

4 Yonah began to go into the city, going a day's journey. And he called out, Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown.

5 And the people of Nineveh believed Creator. They called for a fast and put on sackcloth, from the greatest of them to the least of them.

6 The word reached the king of Nineveh, and he arose from his throne, removed his robe, covered himself with sackcloth, and sat in ashes.

7 And he issued a proclamation and published through Nineveh, By the decree of the king and his nobles: Let neither man nor beast, herd nor flock, taste anything. Let them not feed or drink water,

8 But let man and beast be covered with sackcloth, and let them call out mightily to Creator. Let everyone turn from his evil way and from the violence that is in his hands.

9 Who knows? Creator may turn and relent and turn from his fierce anger, so that we may not perish.

10 When Creator saw what they did, how they turned from their evil way, Creator relented of the disaster that he had said he would do to them, and he did not do it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Chapter 3 in close agreement with the Masoretic. The covenant repentance of Nineveh is the most comprehensive covenant repentance in the prophetic corpus: a pagan Assyrian imperial city responds to a five-word covenant sermon — yet forty days and Nineveh shall be overthrown — with total covenant behavioral reversal, from the king to the cattle. The covenant community that never responded to decades of covenant prophets is shamed by the Assyrians who respond to a single covenant sentence.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 5 — the people of Nineveh believed Creator. The covenant declaration at the center of the chapter: the Ninevites' covenant response begins with covenant belief — emunah — before covenant behavior. The covenant behavioral reversal — fasting, sackcloth, turning from evil — is the covenant expression of covenant belief, not its substitute.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 3. Verse 9 — who knows? Creator may turn and relent. The Assyrian king's covenant theology: the covenant God's response is not mechanically guaranteed even by covenant repentance — who knows? The covenant humility of the pagan king's covenant uncertainty is more covenantally accurate than the presumption of covenant insiders who assume covenant repentance automatically produces covenant mercy.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown. The shortest covenant sermon in the prophetic corpus — five Hebrew words — produces the largest covenant response in the prophetic corpus. The covenant irony is the theological center of the book: the covenant prophet with the longest preparation — the fish belly — delivers the shortest covenant sermon to the audience with the least covenant background — pagan Assyria — and receives the greatest covenant response. Covenant effectiveness is not proportional to covenant eloquence.

YONAH — CHAPTER 4 Yonah Was Exceedingly Displeased — Did I Not Say This? — It Is Better for Me to Die — The Plant — The Worm — Should I Not Pity Nineveh

1 But it displeased Yonah exceedingly, and he was angry.

2 And he prayed to YHWH and said, O YHWH, is not this what I said when I was yet in my country? That is why I made haste to flee to Tarshish, for I knew that you are a gracious Creator and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love, and relenting from disaster.

3 Therefore now, O YHWH, please take my life from me, for it is better for me to die than to live.

4 And YHWH said, Do you do well to be angry?

5 Yonah went out of the city and sat to the east of the city and made a booth for himself there. He sat under it in the shade, till he should see what would become of the city.

6 Now YHWH Creator appointed a plant and made it come up over Yonah, that it might be a shade over his head, to save him from his discomfort. So Yonah was exceedingly glad because of the plant.

7 But when dawn came up the next day, Creator appointed a worm that attacked the plant, so that it withered.

8 When the sun rose, Creator appointed a scorching east wind, and the sun beat down on the head of Yonah so that he was faint. And he asked that he might die and said, It is better for me to die than to live.

9 But Creator said to Yonah, Do you do well to be angry for the plant? And he said, Yes, I do well to be angry, angry enough to die.

10 And YHWH said, You pity the plant, for which you did not labor, nor did you make it grow, which came into being in a night and perished in a night.

11 And should I not pity Nineveh, that great city, in which there are more than 120,000 persons who do not know their right hand from their left, and also much cattle?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Chapter 4 in close agreement with the Masoretic. The final chapter of Yonah is the covenant theological confrontation between YHWH and the covenant prophet about the nature of covenant mercy. Yonah's anger at the covenant mercy shown to Nineveh — verse 1 — is the covenant anger of covenant particularism: covenant mercy extended to covenant outsiders feels to the covenant insider like covenant injustice.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 2 — is not this what I said? I knew that you are gracious and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love. The covenant irony of Yonah's complaint: he accuses YHWH of the covenant character that Hoshea, Yoel, and the entire covenant prophetic tradition celebrate. The covenant attributes of Shemot 34:6 that the covenant community relies on for its own covenant survival are resented when applied to covenant outsiders.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 4. The book ends with a covenant question — verse 11 — should I not pity Nineveh? — that receives no recorded answer. The covenant ending is the covenant hermeneutical device: the reader must answer for Yonah. The covenant question posed to Yonah is posed to every covenant reader who has ever resented covenant mercy extended to covenant enemies.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10-11 — the covenant argument from covenant investment: you pity the plant for which you did not labor. Should I not pity Nineveh with 120,000 who do not know their right hand from their left — and also much cattle? The covenant logic: if Yonah can pity an unearned plant, YHWH can pity a city of covenant ignorant people — and even their cattle. The covenant mercy of YHWH is wider than the covenant prophet's covenant mercy, wider than the covenant community's covenant expectation, wider than covenant deserving.

MIKHAH — DO JUSTICE LOVE KINDNESS WALK HUMBLY

MIKHAH — CHAPTER 1 The Word of YHWH to Mikhah — YHWH Is Coming Out of His Place — For the Transgression of Yaakov — Samaria's Wound Is Incurable — Mourn for the Exile of Your Children

1 The word of YHWH that came to Mikhah of Moreshet in the days of Yotam, Akhaz, and Khizkiyahu, kings of Yehudah, which he saw concerning Samaria and Yerushalayim.

2 Hear, you peoples, all of you. Pay attention, O earth, and all that is in it, and let the Creator YHWH be a witness against you, the Creator from his holy temple.

3 For behold, YHWH is coming out of his place, and will come down and tread upon the high places of the earth.

4 And the mountains will melt under him, and the valleys will split open, like wax before the fire, like waters poured down a steep place.

5 All this is for the transgression of Yaakov and for the sins of the house of Yisra'EL. What is the transgression of Yaakov? Is it not Samaria? And what is the high place of Yehudah? Is it not Yerushalayim?

6 Therefore I will make Samaria a heap in the open country, a place for planting vineyards, and I will pour down her stones into the valley and uncover her foundations.

7 All her carved images shall be beaten to pieces, all her wages shall be burned with fire, and all her idols I will lay waste, for from the fee of a prostitute she gathered them, and to the fee of a prostitute they shall return.

8 For this I will lament and wail. I will go stripped and naked. I will make lamentation like the jackals, and mourning like the ostriches.

9 For her wound is incurable, and it has come to Yehudah. It has reached to the gate of my people, to Yerushalayim.

10 Tell it not in Gat, weep not at all. In Beit-Afrah roll yourselves in the dust.

11 Pass on your way, inhabitants of Shafir, in nakedness and shame. The inhabitants of Za'anan do not come out. The lamentation of Beit-Etzel shall take away from you its standing place.

12 For the inhabitants of Marot wait anxiously for good, because disaster has come down from YHWH to the gate of Yerushalayim.

13 Harness the steeds to the chariots, inhabitants of Lakhish. It was the beginning of sin to the daughter of Tziyon, for in you were found the transgressions of Yisra'EL.

14 Therefore you shall give parting gifts to Moreshet-Gat. The houses of Achziv shall be a deceitful thing to the kings of Yisra'EL.

15 I will again bring a conqueror to you, inhabitants of Mareshshah. The glory of Yisra'EL shall come to Adullam.

16 Make yourselves bald and cut off your hair, for the children of your delight. Make yourselves as bald as the eagle, for they shall go from you into exile.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Mikhah in close agreement with the Masoretic. Chapter 1 opens with a cosmic covenant lawsuit: all peoples and the earth itself are summoned to hear YHWH's covenant testimony against Samaria and Yerushalayim. The covenant theophany — YHWH coming out of his place, mountains melting, valleys splitting — is the covenant context for the covenant indictment of two capital cities that have become the centers of covenant transgression.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 1 in full. Verse 8-9 — for this I will lament and wail. I will go stripped and naked. For her wound is incurable. The covenant prophet's embodied covenant grief: Mikhah mourns like the jackals and ostriches, going stripped and naked, because the covenant wound of Samaria has already reached Yerushalayim. The covenant prophet's grief is the covenant pre-enactment of the covenant community's coming lamentation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 1. Verses 10-16 form a covenant wordplay lament — a covenant poetic sequence in which each city's name echoes a covenant word describing its covenant fate. Gat sounds like tell — do not tell it in tell-town. Beit-Afrah means dust-house — roll in dust there. Shafir means beautiful — go in nakedness and shame. The covenant geographic lament is simultaneously a covenant linguistic covenant testimony.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — what is the transgression of Yaakov? Is it not Samaria? And what is the high place of Yehudah? Is it not Yerushalayim? The covenant identification of the capital cities as the covenant centers of covenant violation: the covenant cities that should have been the covenant centers of covenant fidelity — the covenant royal city and the covenant sanctuary city — are instead the covenant fountainheads of covenant transgression that flows downward through the entire covenant community.

MIKHAH — CHAPTER 2 Woe to Those Who Devise Wickedness — They Covet Fields and Seize Them — Do Not Preach — I Will Assemble All of You — I Will Gather the Remnant

1 Woe to those who devise wickedness and work evil on their beds. When the morning dawns, they perform it, because it is in the power of their hand.

2 They covet fields and seize them, and houses, and take them away. They oppress a man and his house, a man and his inheritance.

3 Therefore thus says YHWH: Behold, against this family I am devising disaster, from which you cannot remove your necks, and you shall not walk haughtily, for it will be a time of disaster.

4 In that day they shall take up a taunt song against you and moan bitterly, and say, We are utterly ruined. He changes the portion of my people. How he removes it from me. To the apostate he allots our fields.

5 Therefore you will have none to cast the line by lot in the assembly of YHWH.

6 Do not preach — thus they preach — one should not preach of such things. Disgrace will not overtake us.

7 Should this be said, O house of Yaakov? Has YHWH grown impatient? Are these his deeds? Do not my words do good to him who walks uprightly?

8 But lately my people have risen up as an enemy. You strip the rich robe from those who pass by trustingly, those who are averse to war.

9 The women of my people you drive out from their delightful houses. From their young children you take away my splendor forever.

10 Arise and go, for this is no place to rest, because of uncleanness that destroys with a grievous destruction.

11 If a man should go about and utter wind and lies, saying, I will preach to you of wine and strong drink, he would be the preacher for this people.

12 I will surely assemble all of you, O Yaakov. I will gather the remnant of Yisra'EL. I will set them together like sheep in a fold, like a flock in its pasture, a noisy multitude of men.

13 He who opens the breach goes up before them. They break through and pass the gate, going out by it. Their king passes on before them, YHWH at their head.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Chapter 2 in close agreement with the Masoretic. The covenant land theft oracle of verses 1-2 is the covenant echo of Eliyahu and Navot's vineyard: those who plan covenant injustice on their beds and execute it at dawn — because they have the power — are the covenant predators of the covenant community's covenant inheritance system. YHWH's covenant response is covenant symmetry: I am devising disaster against you — as you devised iniquity.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 12-13 — I will surely assemble all of you, O Yaakov. I will gather the remnant of Yisra'EL. Their king passes on before them, YHWH at their head. The covenant restoration promise embedded within the covenant judgment chapter: even in the oracle of covenant land dispossession, YHWH inserts the covenant promise of covenant regathering. The covenant King who leads the return is YHWH himself.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 2. Verse 6 — do not preach. Thus they preach. The covenant silencing of the covenant word: the covenant community commands the covenant prophets to stop prophesying judgment. The covenant community's desired covenant preacher — verse 11 — is one who promises wine and strong drink: covenant comfort without covenant truth.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 1-2 — the covenant anatomy of covenant injustice: they devise it on their beds at night — leisure produces covenant planning — they execute it at morning — covenant power enables covenant execution. They covet fields and seize them — the Navot pattern from Melakhim Aleph 21 replicated systematically. The covenant inheritance system of the tribal land grants — established in Yehoshua — is being systematically dismantled by covenant elite land accumulation.

MIKHAH — CHAPTER 3 Hear, You Heads of Yaakov — They Eat the Flesh of My People — The Prophets Who Cry Peace When They Have Something to Eat — Tziyon Shall Be Plowed as a Field

1 And I said, Hear, you heads of Yaakov and rulers of the house of Yisra'EL. Is it not for you to know justice?

2 You who hate the good and love the evil, who tear the skin from off my people and their flesh from off their bones,

3 Who eat the flesh of my people, and flay their skin from off them, and break their bones in pieces and chop them up like meat in a pot, like flesh in a cauldron.

4 Then they will cry to YHWH, but he will not answer them. He will hide his face from them at that time, because they have made their deeds evil.

5 Thus says YHWH concerning the prophets who lead my people astray, who cry Peace when they have something to eat, but declare war against him who puts nothing into their mouths.

6 Therefore it shall be night to you, without vision, and darkness to you, without divination. The sun shall go down on the prophets, and the day shall be black over them.

7 The seers shall be disgraced, and the diviners put to shame. They shall all cover their lips, for there is no answer from Creator.

8 But as for me, I am filled with power, with the Spirit of YHWH, and with justice and might, to declare to Yaakov his transgression and to Yisra'EL his sin.

9 Hear this, you heads of the house of Yaakov and rulers of the house of Yisra'EL, who detest justice and make crooked all that is straight,

10 Who build Tziyon with blood and Yerushalayim with iniquity.

11 Its heads give judgment for a bribe. Its priests teach for a price. Its prophets practice divination for money. Yet they lean on YHWH and say, Is not YHWH in the midst of us? No disaster shall come upon us.

12 Therefore because of you Tziyon shall be plowed as a field. Yerushalayim shall become a heap of ruins, and the mountain of the house a wooded height.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Chapter 3 in close agreement with the Masoretic. The covenant triple indictment of Chapter 3 — leaders, prophets, priests — each indicted for the same covenant sin: using the covenant office for personal financial gain at the covenant community's expense. Verse 11 contains the covenant community's theological self-defense — is not YHWH in the midst of us? No disaster shall come — the covenant temple presence invoked as covenant protection for covenant violators.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 8 — as for me, I am filled with power, with the Spirit of YHWH, and with justice and might, to declare to Yaakov his transgression. The covenant contrast of Mikhah's self-identification against the false prophets: they divine for money; he is filled with YHWH's Spirit. The covenant distinction is not institutional but experiential: the covenant Spirit that fills Mikhah for covenant truth-telling.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 3. Verse 12 — Tziyon shall be plowed as a field, Yerushalayim shall become a heap of ruins, the mountain of the house a wooded height. The covenant prediction cited by the elders in Yirmeyahu 26:18 as the covenant precedent for Yirmeyahu's ministry: Mikhah made the same prediction a century earlier under Hizkiyahu, and Hizkiyahu's covenant repentance produced covenant relenting. The covenant memory of Mikhah's covenant word saved Yirmeyahu's life.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — the prophets who lead my people astray, who cry Peace when they have something to eat, but declare war against him who puts nothing into their mouths. The covenant corruption of the prophetic office is economic at its core: covenant prophecy has become a covenant subscription service. Those who pay receive covenant peace prophecy; those who do not pay receive covenant war prophecy. The covenant word is for sale, and its content is determined by the covenant market.

MIKHAH — CHAPTER 4 In the Latter Days — The Mountain of the House of YHWH — They Shall Beat Their Swords Into Plowshares — I Will Make the Lame a Remnant — You Shall Go to Babylon — YHWH Shall Redeem You

1 It shall come to pass in the latter days that the mountain of the house of YHWH shall be established as the highest of the mountains, and it shall be lifted up above the hills, and peoples shall flow to it.

2 And many nations shall come, and say, Come, let us go up to the mountain of YHWH, to the house of the Creator of Yaakov, that he may teach us his ways and that we may walk in his paths. For out of Tziyon shall go forth the Torah, and the word of YHWH from Yerushalayim.

3 He shall judge between many peoples, and shall decide disputes for strong nations far away. And they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks. Nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore.

4 But they shall sit every man under his vine and under his fig tree, and no one shall make them afraid, for the mouth of YHWH of hosts has spoken.

5 For all the peoples walk each in the name of its god, but we will walk in the name of YHWH our Creator forever and ever.

6 In that day, declares YHWH, I will assemble the lame and gather those who have been driven away and those whom I have afflicted.

7 And the lame I will make the remnant, and those who were cast off, a strong nation, and YHWH will reign over them in Mount Tziyon from this time forth and forevermore.

8 And you, O tower of the flock, hill of the daughter of Tziyon, to you shall it come, the former dominion shall come, kingship for the daughter of Yerushalayim.

9 Now why do you cry aloud? Is there no king in you? Has your counselor perished, that pain seized you like a woman in labor?

10 Writhe and groan, O daughter of Tziyon, like a woman in labor, for now you shall go out from the city and dwell in the open country. You shall go to Babylon. There you shall be rescued. There YHWH will redeem you from the hand of your enemies.

11 Now many nations are assembled against you, saying, Let her be defiled, and let our eyes gaze upon Tziyon.

12 But they do not know the thoughts of YHWH. They do not understand his plan, that he has gathered them as sheaves to the threshing floor.

13 Arise and thresh, O daughter of Tziyon, for I will make your horn iron, and I will make your hoofs bronze. You shall beat in pieces many peoples, and shall devote their gain to YHWH, their wealth to the Sovereign of the whole earth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Chapter 4 in close agreement with the Masoretic. The covenant vision of verses 1-4 — shared with Yesha'yahu 2:2-4 — is the covenant eschatological peace: the covenant mountain exalted, covenant Torah going forth to all nations, covenant military technology repurposed for covenant agriculture. Mikhah uniquely adds verse 4 — every man under his vine and his fig tree — the covenant domestic peace that grounds the cosmic covenant vision in covenant household reality.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 10 — you shall go to Babylon. There you shall be rescued. There YHWH will redeem you from the hand of your enemies. The covenant Babylon prediction: Mikhah in the 8th century BCE predicts the Babylonian exile and the covenant redemption from Babylon — over a century before the exile. The covenant word operates on covenant timelines that exceed covenant human political horizons.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 4. Verse 6-7 — I will assemble the lame and gather those driven away. The lame I will make the remnant, and those cast off, a strong nation. The covenant reversal of the covenant strength criteria: the covenant remnant is constituted from the covenant weak, the covenant lame, the covenant driven away — precisely those whom the covenant community would exclude from covenant strength. The covenant nation that YHWH builds from the broken is stronger than the nation built from the powerful.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — they do not know the thoughts of YHWH. They do not understand his plan, that he has gathered them as sheaves to the threshing floor. The covenant hidden purpose within the covenant catastrophe: the nations that assemble against Tziyon to defeat it are unknowingly being assembled by YHWH for their own covenant judgment. The covenant gathering for covenant attack is simultaneously the covenant gathering for covenant threshing. The covenant enemy's covenant strategy is the covenant instrument of their own covenant defeat.

MIKHAH — CHAPTER 5 From Beit-Lekhem Efratah — One Who Is to Be Ruler in Yisra'EL — He Shall Stand and Shepherd His Flock — The Remnant of Yaakov Among the Nations — I Will Cut Off Sorceries

1 Now muster your troops, O daughter of troops. Siege is laid against us. With a rod they strike the judge of Yisra'EL on the cheek.

2 But you, O Beit-Lekhem Efratah, who are too little to be among the clans of Yehudah, from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Yisra'EL, whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days.

3 Therefore he shall give them up until the time when she who is in labor has given birth. Then the rest of his brothers shall return to the people of Yisra'EL.

4 And he shall stand and shepherd his flock in the strength of YHWH, in the majesty of the name of YHWH his Creator. And they shall dwell secure, for now he shall be great to the ends of the earth.

5 And he shall be their peace. When the Assyrian comes into our land and treads in our palaces, then we will raise against him seven shepherds and eight princes of men.

6 They shall shepherd the land of Assyria with the sword, and the land of Nimrod at its entrances. And he shall deliver us from the Assyrian when he comes into our land and treads within our border.

7 Then the remnant of Yaakov shall be in the midst of many peoples like dew from YHWH, like showers on the grass, which delay not for a man nor wait for the children of man.

8 And the remnant of Yaakov shall be among the nations, in the midst of many peoples, like a lion among the beasts of the forest, like a young lion among the flocks of sheep, which, when it goes through, treads down and tears in pieces, and there is none to deliver.

9 Your hand shall be lifted up over your adversaries, and all your enemies shall be cut off.

10 And in that day, declares YHWH, I will cut off your horses from among you and will destroy your chariots.

11 And I will cut off the cities of your land and throw down all your strongholds.

12 And I will cut off sorceries from your hand, and you shall have no more tellers of fortunes.

13 And I will cut off your carved images and your pillars from among you, and you shall bow down no more to the work of your hands.

14 And I will root out your Asherah images from among you and destroy your cities.

15 And in anger and wrath I will execute vengeance on the nations that did not obey.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Chapter 5 in close agreement with the Masoretic. Verse 2 — from Beit-Lekhem Efratah, too little to be among the clans of Yehudah, from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Yisra'EL, whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days. The covenant origin of the coming covenant ruler is double: from the covenant smallest geographic location — Beit-Lekhem — and from the covenant most ancient temporal origin — from of old, from ancient days.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 4 — he shall stand and shepherd his flock in the strength of YHWH, in the majesty of the name of YHWH his Creator. The covenant shepherd-king from Beit-Lekhem — the city of Dawid the shepherd-king — exercises covenant authority not through covenant military power or covenant political force but through YHWH's covenant strength and covenant name. The covenant ruler's power is covenant delegated, not covenant self-generated.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 5. Verses 10-14 — the covenant purge of the covenant community's covenant trust-substitutes: horses and chariots — covenant military technology replacing covenant trust in YHWH; cities and strongholds — covenant defensive infrastructure; sorceries and fortune-tellers — covenant alternative guidance systems; carved images and pillars — covenant competing worship objects. The coming covenant ruler's covenant community is purged of every covenant alternative to YHWH.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days. The covenant temporal reach of the verse: the coming ruler's covenant origin is not merely Davidic — a dynasty of several centuries — but from ancient days — kedem — the most ancient covenant time. The covenant ruler comes from the covenant origin of the covenant creation order itself. The covenant smallness of Beit-Lekhem and the covenant antiquity of the ruler's origin form the covenant paradox at the heart of the verse: smallest place, most ancient origin.

MIKHAH — CHAPTER 6 Hear What YHWH Says — Arise Plead Your Case — What Does YHWH Require of You — To Do Justice and to Love Kindness — The Statutes of Omri — I Will Make You Sick

1 Hear what YHWH says: Arise, plead your case before the mountains, and let the hills hear your voice.

2 Hear, you mountains, the indictment of YHWH, and you enduring foundations of the earth, for YHWH has an indictment against his people, and he will contend with Yisra'EL.

3 O my people, what have I done to you? How have I wearied you? Answer me.

4 For I brought you up from the land of Egypt and redeemed you from the house of slavery, and I sent before you Mosheh, Aharon, and Miryam.

5 O my people, remember what Balak king of Moav devised, and what Bil'am the son of Be'or answered him, and what happened from Shittim to Gilgal, that you may know the righteous acts of YHWH.

6 With what shall I come before YHWH, and bow myself before Creator on high? Shall I come before him with burnt offerings, with calves a year old?

7 Will YHWH be pleased with thousands of rams, with ten thousands of rivers of oil? Shall I give my firstborn for my transgression, the fruit of my body for the sin of my soul?

8 He has told you, O man, what is good. And what does YHWH require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your Creator?

9 The voice of YHWH cries to the city — and it is sound wisdom to fear your name. Hear of the rod and of him who appointed it.

10 Can I forget any longer the treasures of wickedness in the house of the wicked, and the scant measure that is accursed?

11 Shall I acquit the man with wicked scales and with a bag of deceitful weights?

12 Your rich men are full of violence. Your inhabitants speak lies, and their tongue is deceitful in their mouth.

13 Therefore I strike you with a grievous blow, making you desolate because of your sins.

14 You shall eat, but not be satisfied, and there shall be hunger within you. You shall put away, but not preserve, and what you preserve I will give to the sword.

15 You shall sow, but not reap. You shall tread olives, but not anoint yourselves with oil. You shall tread grapes, but not drink wine.

16 For you have kept the statutes of Omri, and all the works of the house of Akhav. And you have walked in their counsels, that I may make you a desolation, and your inhabitants a hissing. So you shall bear the scorn of my people.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Chapter 6 in close agreement with the Masoretic. Verse 8 — he has told you, O man, what is good. And what does YHWH require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your Creator? The covenant triple requirement is the most quoted verse in Mikhah and one of the most cited in the entire prophetic canon. The covenant reduction of covenant obligation to three covenant essentials: covenant mishpat — justice, covenant hesed — love of kindness, covenant humility before YHWH.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 3-5 — O my people, what have I done to you? How have I wearied you? The covenant self-interrogation of YHWH: before stating the covenant indictment, YHWH asks the covenant community whether they have any covenant grievance against him. The covenant defense of YHWH's covenant character precedes the covenant prosecution of the covenant community's covenant violations.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 6. Verse 7 — shall I give my firstborn for my transgression, the fruit of my body for the sin of my soul? The covenant rhetorical question that excludes child sacrifice: the escalating covenant offerings — burnt offerings, thousands of rams, rivers of oil, firstborn child — reach their covenant impossible limit. YHWH's covenant answer — verse 8 — counters the entire escalating covenant ritual list with three covenant ethical requirements.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 16 — you have kept the statutes of Omri and the works of the house of Akhav. The covenant dynasty of covenant worst practice: Omri and Akhav represent the covenant nadir of the northern monarchy — the covenant introduction of Ba'al worship, the covenant murder of Navot, the covenant alliance with Phoenicia. The southern kingdom in Mikhah's time is following the northern kingdom's covenant worst practices. The covenant community is learning its covenant behavior from the covenant community that was already destroyed.

MIKHAH — CHAPTER 7 Woe Is Me — I Have Become as One Who Gathers Summer Fruit — Do Not Rejoice Over Me, O My Enemy — YHWH Will Bring Me Out to the Light — Who Is a Creator Like You, Pardoning Iniquity

1 Woe is me. For I have become as when the summer fruit has been gathered, as when the grapes have been gleaned. There is no cluster to eat, no first-ripe fig that my soul desires.

2 The godly has perished from the earth, and there is no one upright among mankind. They all lie in wait for blood, and each hunts the other with a net.

3 Their hands are on what is evil, to do it well. The prince and the judge ask for a bribe, and the great man utters the evil desire of his soul. Thus they weave it together.

4 The best of them is like a brier. The most upright of them is sharper than a thorn hedge. The day of your watchmen, of your punishment, has come. Now their confusion is at hand.

5 Put no trust in a neighbor. Have no confidence in a friend. Guard the doors of your mouth from her who lies in your arms.

6 For the son treats the father with contempt, the daughter rises up against her mother, the daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law. A man's enemies are the men of his own house.

7 But as for me, I will look to YHWH. I will wait for the Creator of my salvation. My Creator will hear me.

8 Rejoice not over me, O my enemy. When I fall, I shall rise. When I sit in darkness, YHWH will be a light to me.

9 I will bear the indignation of YHWH because I have sinned against him, until he pleads my cause and executes judgment for me. He will bring me out to the light. I shall look upon his vindication.

10 Then my enemy will see, and shame will cover her who said to me, Where is YHWH your Creator? My eyes will look upon her. Now she will be trampled down like the mire of the streets.

11 A day for the building of your walls. In that day the boundary shall be far extended.

12 In that day they will come to you, from Assyria and the cities of Egypt, and from Egypt to the River, from sea to sea and from mountain to mountain.

13 But the earth will be desolate because of its inhabitants, for the fruit of their deeds.

14 Shepherd your people with your staff, the flock of your inheritance, who dwell alone in a forest in the midst of a garden land, in Bashan and Gil'ad as in the days of old.

15 As in the days when you came out of the land of Egypt, I will show them marvelous things.

16 The nations shall see and be ashamed of all their might. They shall lay their hands on their mouths. Their ears shall be deaf.

17 They shall lick the dust like a serpent, like the crawling things of the earth. They shall come trembling out of their strongholds. They shall turn in dread to YHWH our Creator, and they shall be in fear of you.

18 Who is a Creator like you, pardoning iniquity and passing over transgression for the remnant of his inheritance? He does not retain his anger forever, because he delights in steadfast love.

19 He will again have compassion on us. He will tread our iniquities underfoot. You will cast all our sins into the depths of the sea.

20 You will show faithfulness to Yaakov and steadfast love to Avraham, as you have sworn to our fathers from the days of old.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Chapter 7 in close agreement with the Masoretic. The closing doxology of Mikhah — verses 18-20 — is the covenant climax of the book: Mi-El kamokha — Who is a Creator like you? The covenant rhetorical question that acknowledges YHWH's incomparable covenant character: pardoning iniquity, passing over transgression, not retaining anger forever, delighting in steadfast love. The covenant name Mikhah — who is like YHWH? — is the covenant answer to the covenant question the book asks.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 19-20 — he will cast all our sins into the depths of the sea. You will show faithfulness to Yaakov and steadfast love to Avraham, as you have sworn to our fathers from the days of old. The covenant closing: the covenant sins are not merely forgiven but cast — hurled — into the depths of the sea. The covenant promise grounds covenant restoration in the covenant oath sworn to the covenant fathers — Avraham and Yaakov — making covenant restoration a covenant obligation of YHWH's own covenant integrity.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 7. Verse 8-9 — when I fall, I shall rise. When I sit in darkness, YHWH will be a light to me. I will bear the indignation of YHWH because I have sinned against him, until he pleads my cause. The covenant posture of covenant honest submission: the covenant community acknowledges covenant guilt — I have sinned — while maintaining covenant confidence in covenant advocacy — he will plead my cause. Covenant confession and covenant hope coexist without contradiction.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — a man's enemies are the men of his own house. The covenant social dissolution described by Mikhah: the covenant community's internal covenant trust has collapsed so completely that the covenant family unit itself — the foundational covenant social structure — cannot be trusted. The covenant dissolution that began at the covenant leadership level — Chapter 3 — has reached the covenant household level. Every layer of covenant community has become a covenant threat rather than a covenant refuge.

NAKHUM — THERE IS NO HEALING FOR YOUR WOUND

NAKHUM — CHAPTER 1 The Burden of Nineveh — YHWH Is a Jealous Creator — Who Can Stand Before His Indignation — He Will Make a Full End of His Adversaries — Out of You Came One Who Plotted Evil

1 An oracle concerning Nineveh. The book of the vision of Nakhum of Elkosh.

2 YHWH is a jealous Creator and avenging. YHWH is avenging and wrathful. YHWH takes vengeance on his adversaries and keeps wrath for his enemies.

3 YHWH is slow to anger and great in power, and YHWH will by no means clear the guilty. His way is in whirlwind and storm, and the clouds are the dust of his feet.

4 He rebukes the sea and makes it dry. He dries up all the rivers. Bashan and Karmel wither. The bloom of Lebanon withers.

5 The mountains quake before him. The hills melt. The earth heaves before him, the world and all who dwell in it.

6 Who can stand before his indignation? Who can endure the heat of his anger? His wrath is poured out like fire, and the rocks are broken into pieces by him.

7 YHWH is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble. He knows those who take refuge in him.

8 But with an overflowing flood he will make a complete end of the adversaries, and will pursue his enemies into darkness.

9 What do you plot against YHWH? He will make a complete end. Trouble will not rise up a second time.

10 For they are like entangled thorns, like drunkards as they drink. They are consumed like stubble fully dried.

11 From you came one who plotted evil against YHWH, a worthless counselor.

12 Thus says YHWH, Though they are at full strength and many, they will be cut down and pass away. Though I have afflicted you, I will afflict you no more.

13 And now I will break his yoke from off you and will burst your bonds apart.

14 And YHWH has given commandment about you: No more shall your name be perpetuated. From the house of your gods I will cut off the carved image and the metal image. I will make your grave, for you are vile.

15 Behold, upon the mountains, the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace. Keep your feasts, O Yehudah. Fulfill your vows, for never again shall the worthless pass through you. He is utterly cut off.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Nakhum in close agreement with the Masoretic. Chapter 1 opens with the covenant theophany hymn — verses 2-8 — that establishes YHWH's covenant character: simultaneously covenant jealous and avenging — verse 2 — and covenant good and a stronghold — verse 7. The covenant dual character of YHWH — the same covenant God who destroys adversaries provides refuge for those who take shelter in him — is the covenant theological framework for the entire oracle against Nineveh.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 1 in full. Verse 3 — YHWH is slow to anger and great in power, and YHWH will by no means clear the guilty. The covenant formula draws on the Shemot 34 covenant disclosure but adds the covenant warning that YHWH's covenant patience is not covenant permissiveness: slow to anger — but great in power — but will not clear the guilty. The covenant patience of Yonah's preaching has reached its covenant limit in the generation of Nakhum.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 1. Verse 15 — behold, upon the mountains, the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace. The covenant message of Nineveh's fall is good news for Yehudah: the covenant empire that has oppressed the covenant people for generations is being judged. The covenant irony connecting Yonah and Nakhum: Yonah's mission produced Nineveh's covenant repentance and YHWH's covenant relenting; Nakhum announces that the covenant relenting was not permanent.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2-3 — YHWH is jealous and avenging and wrathful — verse 2 — and slow to anger — verse 3 — the covenant paradox stated without resolution: the covenant attributes of covenant wrath and covenant patience coexist in the same covenant God without contradiction. The covenant answer to the paradox is the covenant timeline: the covenant patience that Yonah witnessed in the Nineveh generation has run its covenant course. The covenant wrath that covenant patience deferred has arrived.

NAKHUM — CHAPTER 2 The Scatterer Has Come Up — The Shield of His Mighty Men Is Red — The Gates of the Rivers Are Opened — Nineveh Is Like a Pool — Wail for Her — YHWH Is Restoring the Majesty of Yaakov

1 The scatterer has come up against you. Man the ramparts. Watch the road. Dress for battle. Collect all your strength.

2 For YHWH is restoring the majesty of Yaakov as the majesty of Yisra'EL, for plunderers have plundered them and ruined their vine branches.

3 The shield of his mighty men is red. His soldiers are clothed in scarlet. The chariots come with flashing metal on the day he musters them. The cypress spears are brandished.

4 The chariots race madly through the streets. They rush to and fro through the squares. They gleam like torches. They dart like lightning.

5 He remembers his officers. They stumble as they go. They hasten to the wall. The siege tower is set up.

6 The river gates are opened. The palace melts away.

7 It is decreed that the city be exiled and carried away. Her slave girls lead her, moaning like doves and beating their breasts.

8 Nineveh is like a pool whose waters run away. Halt. Halt they cry. But none turns back.

9 Plunder the silver. Plunder the gold. There is no end of the treasure, or of the wealth of all precious things.

10 Desolate. Desolation and ruin. Hearts melt and knees tremble. Anguish is in all loins. All faces grow pale.

11 Where is the lions' den, the feeding place of the young lions, where the lion and lioness went, where his cubs were, with none to disturb them?

12 The lion tore enough for his cubs and strangled prey for his lionesses. He filled his caves with prey and his dens with torn flesh.

13 Behold, I am against you, declares YHWH of hosts, and I will burn your chariots in smoke, and the sword shall devour your young lions. I will cut off your prey from the earth, and the voice of your messengers shall no longer be heard.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Chapter 2 in close agreement with the Masoretic. The covenant battle narrative of Chapter 2 is the most cinematically vivid in the prophetic corpus: the pace of the covenant description mirrors the pace of the covenant assault — rapid, fragmented, overwhelming. The covenant irony of verse 1 is total: the city that was the great scatterer of nations is now being told to brace for the scatterer who comes against it.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 2 — YHWH is restoring the majesty of Yaakov as the majesty of Yisra'EL. The covenant purpose of Nineveh's fall is embedded within the description of Nineveh's defeat: the covenant restoration of the covenant people is the covenant reason YHWH fights against the covenant empire. The battle of Chapter 2 is not merely geopolitical but covenantally purposeful.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 2. Verse 11-12 — where is the lions' den? The lion tore enough for his cubs. The covenant retrospective on Nineveh as the great lion-empire: the Assyrian royal iconography of the lion — used extensively in Assyrian palace art — is invoked as the covenant image of Nineveh's predatory covenant power. The covenant lion den is now empty.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — the river gates are opened. The palace melts away. The covenant historical precision: ancient sources report that the fall of Nineveh involved the Khosr River flooding and breaching the city's defenses. The covenant word of Nakhum and the covenant historical record converge at the same covenant detail. The covenant prophecy is not covenant vague but covenant specific in its covenant physical description of the covenant mechanism of covenant judgment.

NAKHUM — CHAPTER 3 Woe to the Bloody City — All for the Countless Whorings of the Prostitute — Are You Better Than No-Amon — Your Nobles Are Like Grasshoppers — There Is No Healing for Your Wound

1 Woe to the bloody city, all full of lies and plunder — no end to the prey.

2 The crack of the whip, and rumble of the wheel, galloping horse and bounding chariot.

3 Horseman charging, flashing sword and glittering spear, hosts of slain, heaps of corpses, dead bodies without end — they stumble over the bodies.

4 And all for the countless whorings of the prostitute, graceful and of deadly charms, who betrays nations with her whorings, and peoples with her charms.

5 Behold, I am against you, declares YHWH of hosts, and will lift up your skirts over your face. And I will make nations look at your nakedness and kingdoms at your shame.

6 I will throw filth at you and treat you with contempt and make you a spectacle.

7 And all who look at you will shrink from you and say, Wasted is Nineveh. Who will grieve for her? Where shall I seek comforters for you?

8 Are you better than No-Amon that sat by the Nile, with water around her, her rampart a sea, and water her wall?

9 Kush was her strength. Egypt too, and that without limit. Put and the Libyan peoples were her helpers.

10 Yet she became an exile. She went into captivity. Her infants were dashed in pieces at the head of every street. For her honored men lots were cast, and all her great men were bound in chains.

11 You also will be drunken. You will go into hiding. You will seek a refuge from the enemy.

12 All your fortresses are like fig trees with first-ripe figs — if shaken they fall into the mouth of the eater.

13 Behold, your troops are women in your midst. The gates of your land are wide open to your enemies. Fire has devoured your bars.

14 Draw water for the siege. Strengthen your forts. Go into the clay. Tread the mortar. Take hold of the brick mold.

15 There the fire will devour you. The sword will cut you off. It will devour you like the locust. Multiply yourselves like the locust. Multiply like the grasshopper.

16 You increased your merchants more than the stars of the heavens. The locust spreads its wings and flies away.

17 Your princes are like grasshoppers, your scribes like clouds of locusts settling on the fences in a day of cold — when the sun rises, they fly away. No one knows where they are.

18 Your shepherds are asleep, O king of Assyria. Your nobles slumber. Your people are scattered on the mountains with none to gather them.

19 There is no healing for your wound. Your injury is fatal. All who hear the news about you clap their hands over you. For upon whom has not come your unceasing evil?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QXII preserves Chapter 3 in close agreement with the Masoretic. The closing oracle against Nineveh employs the prostitute metaphor — as Hoshea and Yehezkel employed it for Yisra'EL — but now directed at the covenant empire: Nineveh's political seductions and covenant betrayals of nations are the covenant harlotry for which the covenant judgment falls. The covenant cosmic prostitute who sold herself to empire receives the covenant judgment of exposure and shame.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 8-10 — are you better than No-Amon — Thebes — that sat by the Nile, with Kush and Egypt and Put and Libya as her helpers? Yet she became an exile. The covenant historical precedent: the Assyrians themselves destroyed the great Egyptian city of Thebes in 663 BCE. Now Nakhum turns the covenant precedent against Nineveh: the same covenant fate that Nineveh inflicted on Thebes will come upon Nineveh.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic and DSS are in close agreement for Chapter 3. Verse 19 — there is no healing for your wound. Your injury is fatal. All who hear the news about you clap their hands over you. For upon whom has not come your unceasing evil? The final covenant verse of Nakhum ends not with covenant lament but with covenant universal acknowledgment: every nation that suffered under Assyrian covenant brutality applauds the covenant judgment. The covenant reaction of the covenant nations to Nineveh's fall is the covenant measure of the covenant evil done.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The book of Nakhum presents a profound covenant theological challenge for modern readers: it is entirely a covenant oracle of judgment with no covenant call to repentance directed at the covenant enemy. The covenant comfort it offers is entirely to the covenant oppressed — Yehudah under Assyrian threat. The covenant answer is that covenant justice must eventually reach covenant power that has operated outside covenant accountability. The covenant patience expressed in Yonah's mission was real; its exhaustion expressed in Nakhum's vision is equally real.

END OF PART THREE — OVADYAH · YONAH · MIKHAH · NAKHUM
